***Citrus unshiu*** is a semi-seedless and easy-peeling [citrus](/wiki/Citrus "Citrus") species, also known as the **satsuma mandarin** or **Japanese mandarin**.[[1]](#cite_note-sortingcitrus-1)

## Nomenclature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Citrus_unshiu&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Nomenclature")]

The name *unshiu* is written with the same Chinese characters in both Chinese and Japanese ([traditional Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters "Traditional Chinese characters"): 溫州蜜柑; [simplified Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters "Simplified Chinese characters") and [Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_language "Japanese language"): 温州蜜柑; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *wēnzhōu mìgān*; [rōmaji](/wiki/Romanization_of_Japanese "Romanization of Japanese"): *unshū mikan*), and in both languages it means "honey citrus of [Wenzhou](/wiki/Wenzhou "Wenzhou")."

An alternative Chinese name, *wúhé jú* ([simplified Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters "Simplified Chinese characters"): 无核橘; [traditional Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters "Traditional Chinese characters"): 無核橘), means "seedless [mandarin](/wiki/Mandarin_orange "Mandarin orange")".

One of the English names for the fruit, *satsuma*, is derived from the former [Satsuma Province](/wiki/Satsuma_Province "Satsuma Province") in Japan, from which these fruits were first exported to the West.[[2]](#cite_note-florida-2)

The [Afrikaans](/wiki/Afrikaans "Afrikaans") name *naartjie* is also used in [South African English](/wiki/South_African_English "South African English"). It came originally from the [Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_language "Tamil language") word *nartei*, meaning citrus.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Classification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Citrus_unshiu&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Classification")]

Under the [Tanaka classification system](/wiki/Citrus_taxonomy "Citrus taxonomy"), *Citrus unshiu* is considered a separate species from the [mandarin](/wiki/Mandarin_orange "Mandarin orange"). Under the [Swingle system](/wiki/Citrus_taxonomy "Citrus taxonomy"), unshius are considered to be a group of mandarin varieties.[[4]](#cite_note-identification-4) Genetic analysis has shown the Satsuma to be a highly inbred mandarin-[pomelo](/wiki/Pomelo "Pomelo") hybrid, with 22% of its genome, a larger proportion than seen in most mandarins, coming from pomelo. It arose when a mandarin of the low-pomelo Huanglingmiao or kishumikan variety (placed in *C. reticulata* by Tanaka) was crossed with a pomelo or pomelo hybrid, then the resulting cultivar was [backcrossed](/wiki/Backcross "Backcross") with another Huanglingmiao or kishumikan mandarin.[[5]](#cite_note-naro-5)[[6]](#cite_note-Talon-6)[[7]](#cite_note-Shimizu-7)

## Characteristics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Citrus_unshiu&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Characteristics")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Chenpi%2C%E9%99%B3%E7%9A%AE%2CCitrus_unshiu_5026687.JPG/250px-Chenpi%2C%E9%99%B3%E7%9A%AE%2CCitrus_unshiu_5026687.JPG)](/wiki/File:Chenpi,%E9%99%B3%E7%9A%AE,Citrus_unshiu_5026687.JPG)

The dried peel is used in [Chinese cuisine](/wiki/Chinese_cuisine "Chinese cuisine").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/%E3%81%BF%E3%81%8B%E3%82%93_%28311974839%29.jpg/250px-%E3%81%BF%E3%81%8B%E3%82%93_%28311974839%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E3%81%BF%E3%81%8B%E3%82%93_(311974839).jpg)

Satsuma orange trees in Izunokuni, Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Citrus_unshiu_20101118_b.jpg/250px-Citrus_unshiu_20101118_b.jpg)](/wiki/File:Citrus_unshiu_20101118_b.jpg)

Satsuma orange fruits

*Citrus unshiu* is one of the sweetest citrus varieties.[[8]](#cite_note-sweetest-8) It is usually seedless, and is about the size of other [mandarin oranges](/wiki/Mandarin_orange "Mandarin orange") (*Citrus reticulata*). Satsumas are known for their loose, leathery skin; the fruit is very easily peeled in comparison to other citrus fruits.[[9]](#cite_note-:2-9) The rind is often smooth to slightly rough with the shape of a medium to small flattened sphere.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-ufl-11) Satsumas usually have 10 to 12 easily separable segments with tough membranes.[[11]](#cite_note-ufl-11) The flesh is particularly delicate, and cannot withstand the effects of careless handling.[[9]](#cite_note-:2-9) Coloring of the fruit is often dependent on climate; satsumas grown in humid areas may be ripe while the skin is still green while those grown in areas with cool night temperatures may see a brilliant reddish orange skin at peak.[[11]](#cite_note-ufl-11)

Satsumas are cold-hardy, and when planted in colder locations, the fruit becomes sweeter from the colder temperatures. A mature satsuma tree can survive down to −9 °C (15 °F) or even −11 °C (12 °F) for a few hours.[[12]](#cite_note-stein-12) Of the edible citrus varieties, only the [kumquat](/wiki/Kumquat "Kumquat") is more cold-hardy. Satsumas rarely have any thorns, an attribute that also makes them popular.[[11]](#cite_note-ufl-11) They can be grown from seed, which takes about eight years until the first fruits are produced, or grafted onto other [citrus rootstocks](/wiki/Citrus_rootstock "Citrus rootstock"), such as [trifoliate orange](/wiki/Trifoliate_orange "Trifoliate orange").[[11]](#cite_note-ufl-11)

Various [cultivars](/wiki/Cultivars "Cultivars") have been developed based on the *Citrus unshiu*, and in Japan, three cultivars, namely miyagawa wase, okitsu wase, and aoshima unshu, account for nearly half of the production volume of *Citrus unshiu*.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Citrus_unshiu&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: History")]

### Origins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Citrus_unshiu&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Origins")]

Historically, there are two main theories regarding the origin of *Citrus unshiu*: one proposing a Japanese origin and the other a Chinese origin.[[2]](#cite_note-florida-2)[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-crosscurrents.hawaii.edu-15) The species was named after [Unshu (Wenzhou)](/wiki/Wenzhou "Wenzhou"), a famous production area of *Citrus* species in China, in the late Edo period of Japan.[[16]](#cite_note-jbp-16)

Genetic analyses by Chinese scientists generally support that *Citrus unshiu* (unshiu mandarin) arose through natural hybridisation among Chinese mandarin lineages. One genomic study inferred 'Ruju' as the seed parent and 'Bendiguang' as the pollen parent, and proposed eastern Zhejiang, especially the Huangyan area of Taizhou, as a plausible origin because related cultivated mandarins have long co-occurred there and the region has a documented history of mandarin cultivation spanning more than 1,700 years.[[17]](#cite_note-:1-17)

Genetic studies by Japanese scientists suggest that the maternal parent of *Citrus unshiu* is kishu (*Citrus kinokuni*) and the paternal parent is kunenbo (*Citrus nobilis* Lour. var. *kunip*).[[18]](#cite_note-plos-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[5]](#cite_note-naro-5)

### History in Japan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Citrus_unshiu&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: History in Japan")]

After its formation in China, mandarin was probably introduced to Japan through cultural exchange and maritime trade from the [Tang dynasty](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty") onwards. Historical records and genomic evidence suggest that the cultivar later diversified in Japan primarily through [somatic mutations](/wiki/Somatic_mutation "Somatic mutation"), resulting in the wide variety of modern Satsuma mandarins cultivated today.[[17]](#cite_note-:1-17)

According to the Japanese origin theory, the species *citrus unshiu* emerged in Nishi-Nakajima, Higo Province (later [Nagashima, Kagoshima](/wiki/Nagashima,_Kagoshima "Nagashima, Kagoshima")), in the 1600s as a result of parent species introduced from China. This theory is supported by the [Japanese Ministry of Agriculture, Forestry and Fisheries](/wiki/Ministry_of_Agriculture,_Forestry_and_Fisheries_(Japan) "Ministry of Agriculture, Forestry and Fisheries (Japan)"),[[20]](#cite_note-maff-20) [Ehime Prefecture](/wiki/Ehime_Prefecture "Ehime Prefecture"),[[21]](#cite_note-ehime-21) and several Japanese scientists.[[18]](#cite_note-plos-18)[[22]](#cite_note-Misaki-22) Before the name *unshu mikan* was established, it was locally known as *nakajima mikan* or *nagashima mikan* after the location of its purported birth.[[22]](#cite_note-Misaki-22)[[16]](#cite_note-jbp-16)[[20]](#cite_note-maff-20)[[21]](#cite_note-ehime-21)

During the [Edo period](/wiki/Edo_period "Edo period"), [kishu mikans](/wiki/Kishu_mikan "Kishu mikan") remained the dominant variety due to a popular superstition that consuming the seedless *Citrus unshiu* made an individual prone to infertility. It was only after the modernisation of the [Meiji period](/wiki/Meiji_period "Meiji period") that *Citrus unshiu* surged in popularity,[[16]](#cite_note-jbp-16) eventually becoming closely associated with Japanese winter culture and the use of [kotatsu](/wiki/Kotatsu "Kotatsu").[[23]](#cite_note-23)

### Spread to the United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Citrus_unshiu&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Spread to the United States")]

[Jesuits](/wiki/Society_of_Jesus "Society of Jesus") brought the fruit from Asia to North America in the 18th century, establishing groves at the Jesuit Plantation upriver from [New Orleans, Louisiana](/wiki/New_Orleans,_Louisiana "New Orleans, Louisiana") (then part of [New Spain](/wiki/New_Spain "New Spain")). The municipal street "Orange" in New Orleans was originally named "Rue Des Orangers" after this site. These groves were later relocated further south to [Plaquemines Parish, Louisiana](/wiki/Plaquemines_Parish,_Louisiana "Plaquemines Parish, Louisiana"), to provide better protection from frost; the Becnel family remains the largest citrus growers in the region today.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

The fruit became significantly more common in the United States during the late 19th century. In 1878, Owari mikans were brought from the [Satsuma region](/wiki/Satsuma_province "Satsuma province") of Japan to the United States by Anna Van Valkenburgh, the wife of General [Robert B. Van Valkenburgh](/wiki/Robert_B._Van_Valkenburgh "Robert B. Van Valkenburgh"), the US Minister to Japan. She renamed the fruit "satsumas" after their region of origin.[[2]](#cite_note-florida-2)[[25]](#cite_note-:0-25) Between 1908 and 1911, approximately one million Owari mikan trees were imported and planted across the lower Gulf Coast states.[[12]](#cite_note-stein-12) Owari is still commonly grown in [Florida](/wiki/Florida "Florida").[[11]](#cite_note-ufl-11) Several towns, including [Satsuma, Alabama](/wiki/Satsuma,_Alabama "Satsuma, Alabama"), [Satsuma, Florida](/wiki/Satsuma,_Florida "Satsuma, Florida"), [Satsuma, Texas](/wiki/Satsuma,_Texas "Satsuma, Texas"), and [Satsuma, Louisiana](/wiki/Satsuma,_Louisiana "Satsuma, Louisiana"), were named after the fruit. By 1920, [Jackson County, Florida](/wiki/Jackson_County,_Florida "Jackson County, Florida"), had declared itself the "Satsuma Capital of the World". However, the commercial industry suffered major setbacks due to a −13.3 °C (8.1 °F) cold snap in 1911, a hurricane in 1915,[[12]](#cite_note-stein-12) and severe freezes in the late 1930s.

## Distribution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Citrus_unshiu&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Distribution")]

*Citrus unshiu* is amongst others grown in Japan, Spain, central China, Korea, the US, South Africa, South America, New Zealand, and around the [Black Sea](/wiki/Black_Sea "Black Sea").[[11]](#cite_note-ufl-11)[[25]](#cite_note-:0-25)

## Varieties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Citrus_unshiu&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Varieties")]

*Unshiu* varieties cluster among the [mandarin](/wiki/Mandarin_orange "Mandarin orange") family.[[26]](#cite_note-ident1-26) There are, however, some [hybrids](/wiki/Citrus_hybrids "Citrus hybrids").

### Possible non-hybrids

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Citrus_unshiu&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Possible non-hybrids")]

* [Kishu mikan](/wiki/Kishu_mikan "Kishu mikan")
* [Ōgonkan](/wiki/%C5%8Cgonkan "Ōgonkan") or [Ki-mikan](/wiki/Ki-mikan "Ki-mikan")
* [Komikan](/wiki/Komikan_(fruit) "Komikan (fruit)")

### Hybrids

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Citrus_unshiu&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Hybrids")]

* [Amanatsu](/wiki/Amanatsu "Amanatsu") (pomelo hybrid)
* [Kinkoji unshiu](/wiki/Kinkoji_unshiu "Kinkoji unshiu") (*C. obovoidea*(kinkoji) × *C. unshiu*)[[27]](#cite_note-27)
* [Kiyomi](/wiki/Kiyomi "Kiyomi")
  + [Dekopon](/wiki/Dekopon "Dekopon") is a kiyomi hybrid
* [Kobayashi mikan](/wiki/Kobayashi_mikan "Kobayashi mikan") (*C. natsudaidai* × *C.unshiu*)[[28]](#cite_note-ident2-28)
* [Shonan Gold](/wiki/Shonan_Gold "Shonan Gold")

Although mandarins were a luxury, unshu mikan were not the most popular. The unshu mikan is unique in that it is ready to peel and has no seeds. The lack of seeds is good because they are easy to eat, but in the Edo period, the lack of seeds was a factor that made them unpopular. It was believed that eating seedless fruit meant that one could no longer produce offspring, thus ending one's family lineage. When the fruit was named "unshu mikan" in the late Edo period, it was finally recognised. Wenzhou is a mandarin production area in China, and the name "unshu mikan" means "a mandarin comparable to the one produced in Wenzhou".
